analysis:
Across the stronger heuristics (1–5, 7–9) a common, effective pipeline emerges: (1) deterministic Pareto prefiler, (2) cheap vectorized proxies (box-volume ± multi-weight scalarization) to rank and form an O(k)-shortlist, (3) a greedy/CELF lazy-greedy core that uses cached singleton HVs (and selective pairwise HVs when shortlist is small), and (4) a bounded local 1-for-1 swap cleanup. Strengths: heavy use of vectorized precomputation (diffs, rect_vol), adaptive shortlist sizing, timestamped lazy evaluations to avoid recomputing marginal gains, deterministic tie-breakers, and explicit caps on HV calls or swaps. Heuristic 1 stands out by doing a tiny shortlist greedy with exact HV gains and then an incremental cached-swap pass — minimizing expensive HV computations while guaranteeing exactly k unique points.

Weak/losing variants (10–21) tend to hurt performance by either (a) leaning too heavily on expensive HV calls without sufficient pruning or caching, (b) enforcing overly strict HV-call budgets or tiny shortlists that miss valuable points, or (c) adding complex bookkeeping and many Python-level loops that duplicate work (repeated vstack/pg.hypervolume constructions, membership checks, repeated argsorts), increasing overhead. Common avoidable costs: constructing a new pygmo.hypervolume wrapper per HV call, repeated recomputation of ref−points/diffs, excessive shortlist expansion and full-array argsorts in inner loops, and frequent Python-level set/list membership checks inside hot loops. Good data-reuse patterns seen in top heuristics are: keep precomputed ref-diffs/rect-vol, store hv_single and pair_hv caches, use CELF timestamps to postpone HV recalculation, and update selected-structures in-place to avoid repeated vstack/allocations.
experience: Design next-gen heuristics around a small adaptive shortlist, CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton (and selective pairwise) HVs, tight box‑volume upper‑bound pruning, and a bounded deterministic swap pass. Vectorize all proxy computations, minimize per-call object creation (reuse arrays/pg objects if possible), and tune shortlist size/HV-budget empirically. Prefer a few exact HV calls over many approximate ones; avoid Python-level loops in hot paths.
current_reflection: Design next-gen heuristics around a small adaptive shortlist, CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton (and selective pairwise) HVs, tight box‑volume upper‑bound pruning, and a bounded deterministic swap pass. Vectorize all proxy computations, minimize per-call object creation (reuse arrays/pg objects if possible), and tune shortlist size/HV-budget empirically. Prefer a few exact HV calls over many approximate ones; avoid Python-level loops in hot paths.
good_reflections: Prioritize reducing exact HV calls: build a high-precision O(k) shortlist with cheap proxies, cache singleton (and small pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates and box‑bound pruning, then run a tiny deterministic swap pass. Add an explicit HV-call budget with graceful fallback approximations and vectorized nondomination/precomputation to get consistent, fast, and high-quality k‑subsets.

Keep the four-stage pipeline, but (a) vectorize everything possible (precompute ref−pts, rect volumes), (b) use sets/boolean masks not list membership for O(1) checks, (c) CELF+singleton cache+selective pairwise cache minimises HV calls, (d) use an adaptive O(k) shortlist (cap by Pareto size), and (e) expose an HV-call budget and an approximate HVC fallback (many-weight scalarization) to trade quality vs time deterministically.

Prefer a compact, high‑precision shortlist (fast HVC proxy) + CELF with singleton (and tiny pairwise) HV caches. Precompute ref−points and box volumes, use vectorized Pareto filtering, lexicographic stable ordering, and sets for membership. Lazily recompute true marginal gains only when upper bounds indicate promise, cap HV calls, and prune swap checks by box/HVC upper bounds. These give reproducible, high‑quality k‑subsets with far fewer expensive hypervolume evaluations.

Design heuristics to minimize exact HV evaluations. Use strong, vectorized proxies to form an adaptive O(k) shortlist, run CELF with singleton caches and timestamps, selectively cache pairwise HVs only for tiny shortlists, prune by cheap upper bounds, maintain preallocated selected buffers and boolean masks/sets, and enforce a strict HV‑call budget plus small deterministic swap budget. This yields near‑optimal HV per cost while keeping runtime predictable.

Favor a CELF lazy greedy on a well‑constructed adaptive shortlist (box_vol × scalarization) with cached singleton and selective pairwise HVs, tight box-based upper‑bound pruning, and an hv-call budget scaled by k and shortlist size. Use vectorized dominance checks, sets for membership, incremental HV updates (avoid repeated vstack), and a small bounded deterministic swap pass. Parallelize expensive HV evaluations where possible.
bad_reflections: Design shortlists adaptively (k-proportional, diversity-aware), use CELF with cached singletons and timestamps, prune with cheap box-volume upper bounds, limit pairwise caches to tiny shortlists, and enforce an explicit HV-call budget. Reuse precomputed ref-diffs and avoid Python-level O(n) membership/reshaping in inner loops; prefer boolean masks and batched NV operations. These changes yield most HV-quality gains per unit runtime.

Prefer a Pareto‑filtered, vectorized scoring to form an O(k) shortlist; run CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton (and selective pair) HVs, prune via cheap box‑volume upper bounds, enforce an HV‑call budget, and finish with a tiny deterministic swap pass. Optimize data reuse (index slicing, sets for membership), minimize array copies, and adapt shortlist size to pareto/k to balance quality and HV cost.

Prioritize designs that (1) aggressively prune with cheap proxies, (2) limit exact HV calls via CELF + cached singletons, (3) precompute vectorized diffs and reuse arrays, (4) bound shortlist size and swap iterations, and (5) avoid per‑candidate object/allocation churn (reuse hypervolume objects or batch HV calls). Small, well‑chosen weight sets and selective pairwise caching yield high ROI.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: adaptive O(k) shortlist, CELF lazy greedy, singleton/pairwise HV cache, box‑volume upper bounds, HV‑call budget, vectorization, deterministic swap.
- Advice: build a fast high‑precision shortlist via vectorized proxies, CELF with cached singleton (and tiny selective pairwise) HVs, prune candidates by tight box bounds, cap HV calls and finish with a bounded deterministic swap; preallocate/reuse arrays and HV objects; use sets/boolean masks for membership.
- Avoid: many repeated exact HV calls, Python‑level loops in hot paths, per‑candidate object allocation, large pairwise caches, and non‑vectorized dominance checks.
- Explanation: this minimizes expensive hypervolume evaluations, yields consistent high‑quality k‑subsets, and keeps runtime predictable and tunable via the HV budget and shortlist size.
